metadata.title: UPSC Mains GS Paper 4 - Ethics, Integrity & Aptitude Comprehensive Syllabus
metadata.purpose: Complete syllabus for MentorsDaily UPSC portal — AI module generation
metadata.note: Ethics GS4 is unique — 40% theory + 60% case studies in actual paper. Module design reflects this split.

book_1.title: Lexicon for Ethics, Integrity & Aptitude for IAS General Studies Paper IV
book_1.author: N.B. Singh
book_1.usage: Primary theory reference — ethics concepts, thinkers, definitions

book_2.title: Ethics, Integrity and Aptitude
book_2.author: G. Subba Rao & P.N. Roy Chowdhury
book_2.usage: Case studies, civil services values, applied ethics in governance

book_3.title: 2nd ARC Report 4 — Ethics in Governance
book_3.usage: Probity in governance, conflicts of interest, public accountability frameworks

book_5.title: Work, Life and Values — NCERT Class 11 Psychology
book_5.usage: Attitude formation, emotional intelligence, motivation theory

section_A.description: Theory questions — ethics, aptitude, thinkers, governance values

section_B.description: Case Studies — 4-6 case studies based on real governance scenarios

upsc_gs4_paper_pattern.key_insight: Strong conceptual grounding directly enables better case study answers — theory and application are inseparable in GS4

topics.topic_name: Essence and Determinants of Ethics
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 1 — Nature and Scope of Ethics; Chapter 2 — Determinants of Ethics
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch 1 — Political Theory: An Introduction (normative questions)
key_concepts: Ethics defined — systematic study of right conduct, values, moral principles
Descriptive vs Normative ethics — what IS vs what OUGHT TO BE
Meta-ethics — study of nature, foundations, and methodology of ethics
Applied ethics — ethics in specific domains (medical, business, public policy)
Three determinants: Intention (motive), Means (methods), End (outcome)
Consequences vs intentions — consequentialism vs deontology in one frame
Social vs individual ethics — personal morality vs public morality
Relativism vs universalism — cultural variation vs universal moral truths
Moral philosophy vs law — ethics as internal constraint, law as external
Evolution of ethics — Greek origins (ethos), Roman (mores), modern frameworks
Ethics in everyday life — family, profession, society, governance
Moral facts — can ethical claims be objectively true or false?
Relationship between ethics, law, and public policy
Ethics as foundation for integrity in public life
Importance of ethics in UPSC context — civil servant decision-making
topics.case_study_link: Scenarios where civil servant must choose between legal compliance and ethical action

topics.topic_name: Dimensions of Ethics — Personal, Social, Organisational
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 3 — Dimensions of Ethics; Subba Rao Ch 2 — Ethics in Public Life
topics.ncert_reference: NCERT Class 12 Sociology Ch 1 — Structural Change (social norms formation)
key_concepts: Personal ethics — individual moral code, conscience, character virtues
Social ethics — obligations to community, fairness, justice, rights
Professional ethics — norms of conduct in specific profession (medicine, law, civil services)
Organisational ethics — values embedded in institutional culture
Political ethics — ethics of power, representation, accountability
Environmental ethics — moral obligations to non-human nature
Tension between personal ethics and professional duty
Whistleblowing as ethical act vs institutional loyalty
Collective vs individual moral responsibility
Role morality — can an official act differently from personal morality?
Public ethics vs private ethics — why distinction matters for governance
Institutional integrity — how organisations develop ethical culture
Ethics of care — relationships, empathy, particular obligations
Civic virtue — citizenship duties and democratic ethics
Interdependence of dimensions — personal ethics shapes public conduct
topics.pyq_frequency: High — frequently tested in theory section
topics.case_study_link: IAS officer scenario: personal religious belief vs official secularism duty

topics.topic_name: Human Values — Learning from Family, Society, Educational Institutions
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 4 — Human Values and Their Sources; Subba Rao Ch 4 — Sources of Ethical Guidance
key_concepts: Values defined — enduring beliefs that guide conduct and judgement
Instrumental vs terminal values — Rokeach Value Survey framework
Value formation process — family socialisation, peer influence, education
Role of family in value inculcation — primary agent of socialisation
Role of educational institutions — moral education, character building
Role of society and culture — community norms, religious traditions
Role of media in shaping values — positive (civic awareness) and negative (materialism)
Value conflict — when values clash in real situations
Value priorities — hierarchy of values in decision-making
Universal human values — dignity, justice, compassion, truth
Indian value traditions — dharma, karma, ahimsa, satya
Western value traditions — liberty, equality, fraternity
Values vs norms — abstract principles vs concrete behavioural expectations
Good Samaritan ethics — obligation to help vs personal safety
Moral development — Kohlberg's stages applied to civil servants
topics.pyq_frequency: High — appears in both theory and case study format
topics.case_study_link: Family pressure scenario on a civil servant deciding a contract award

topics.topic_name: Role of Family, Society and Educational Institutions in Inculcating Values
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 4 Section B; 2nd ARC Report 4 — Chapter 2 on Value Formation
key_concepts: Family as first school of ethics — parental example vs explicit teaching
Socialisation theory — Berger and Luckmann social construction of reality
Schools and moral education — value-based curriculum, NCC, scouting
Peer groups as value shapers — conformity and moral courage
Religious institutions — moral frameworks, compassion, service ethic
Media literacy — critical engagement with value messages
Civil society organisations — NGOs, community groups fostering civic values
State role in value education — curriculum design, teacher training
Sports and games — teamwork, fair play, resilience as value training
Literature and arts — humanities as moral imagination development
Role models and mentors — learning ethics through observation
Negative socialisation — corruption, nepotism learned in same institutions
Generational value shift — postmaterialist values (Inglehart)
Dignity of labour — value that must be consciously taught
Ethics training in civil services — LBSNAA curriculum design
topics.case_study_link: Designing ethics curriculum for a government training institute

sample_questions: What do you understand by the essence of ethics? Explain the determinants that shape ethical behaviour in individuals. (10 marks)
Distinguish between personal ethics and professional ethics. In what situations can they conflict? How should a civil servant resolve such conflicts? (12.5 marks)
Case Study: A DM discovers that the contract for building a flood shelter was given to a firm owned by his cousin under previous collector. The project quality is good. What should he do? (20 marks)

topics.topic_name: Consequentialism — Utilitarianism
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 6 — Consequentialist Ethics; Subba Rao Ch 5 — Ethical Theories
key_concepts: Consequentialism — rightness judged by outcomes
Act Utilitarianism — Jeremy Bentham, each act judged by its consequences
Rule Utilitarianism — Mill, rules that generally maximise utility
Hedonistic calculus — Bentham's 7 factors: intensity, duration, certainty, etc.
Mill's higher and lower pleasures — quality matters, not just quantity
Utility principle — greatest happiness for the greatest number
Strengths: practical, measurable, democratic in treating all equally
Weaknesses: ignores minority rights, allows unjust means for good ends
Preference utilitarianism — Peter Singer, satisfying actual preferences
Rule vs Act tension — trolley problem applied to governance
Policy applications — cost-benefit analysis in public policy
Consequentialist justification for civil disobedience
Corruption as consequentialist fallacy — 'good outcome justifies bribe'
Critique from rights theory — some acts wrong regardless of outcome
Application in UPSC context — resource allocation decisions

topics.topic_name: Deontological Ethics — Kant's Categorical Imperative
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 7 — Deontological Ethics; Subba Rao Ch 5
key_concepts: Deontology — rightness based on duty, regardless of consequences
Immanuel Kant — Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
Categorical Imperative — unconditional moral obligation
First formulation: Universalisability — act only as you would want all to act
Second formulation: Humanity formula — treat persons as ends, never merely means
Third formulation: Kingdom of Ends — act as legislator in moral community
Perfect duties vs imperfect duties — lying never allowed vs charity when possible
Good will as the only unconditional good — motivation matters most
Rights-based ethics — deontological foundation for human rights
Rule of law as deontological governance — rules applied without discretion
Strengths: respects human dignity, consistent, rights-protective
Weaknesses: rigid, ignores consequences, conflicting duties problem
Ross's Prima Facie duties — fidelity, reparation, gratitude, non-maleficence
Application: civil servant cannot lie even to protect minor from harm
Deontological basis for bureaucratic neutrality and impartiality
topics.pyq_frequency: High — Kant asked directly multiple times

topics.topic_name: Virtue Ethics — Aristotle and Indian Traditions
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 8 — Virtue Ethics; Chapter 14 — Indian Ethical Traditions
key_concepts: Virtue ethics — focus on character rather than acts or consequences
Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics — eudaimonia (flourishing) as highest good
Virtues as golden mean — courage between cowardice and recklessness
Cardinal virtues — prudence, justice, fortitude, temperance
Intellectual vs moral virtues — reason vs character
Habituation — virtues are learned through practice, not innate
Phronesis (practical wisdom) — knowing the right thing in context
Contemporary revival — Alasdair MacIntyre, Martha Nussbaum
Indian virtue ethics — dharma as virtue concept in Bhagavad Gita
Nishkama karma — duty without attachment to fruits (Gita Ch 3)
Gandhi's ethical framework — truth, non-violence, self-discipline as virtues
Buddha's Eightfold Path — virtue ethics structure in Buddhist philosophy
Mahavira's anekantavada — multiple perspectives as epistemic virtue
Strengths: holistic, context-sensitive, accounts for moral character
Weakness: circular — virtuous is what virtuous person does
Application: civil service character-building, recruitment criteria
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — Indian thinkers always feature

topics.topic_name: Other Ethical Frameworks — Contractarianism, Care Ethics, Rawls
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 9 — Social Contract Theory; Chapter 10 — Justice Theories
key_concepts: Social Contract Theory — Hobbes, Locke, Rousseau on origins of political obligation
Hobbes — state of nature, sovereign power, security as primary value
Locke — natural rights, government by consent, right to revolution
Rousseau — general will, popular sovereignty, civic virtue
Rawls' Theory of Justice — original position and veil of ignorance
Difference Principle — inequalities justified only if they benefit least advantaged
Equal basic liberties — first principle of justice
Fairness as justice — procedural justice framework
Nozick's libertarian response — rights as side constraints, minimal state
Amartya Sen's capabilities approach — freedom as real capabilities
Care Ethics — Carol Gilligan, Nel Noddings — relationships and empathy
Feminist critique of impartial ethics — contextualised, relational decision-making
Contractarian basis for reservation policy and affirmative action
Environmental justice — Rawls extended to future generations
Application in UPSC: welfare state justification, social justice schemes

sample_questions: Compare and contrast Consequentialism and Deontological ethics. Which framework is more suitable for civil servants? (12.5 marks)
Explain Kant's Categorical Imperative with suitable examples. How does it apply to public service? (10 marks)
What is Virtue Ethics? How do Indian philosophical traditions contribute to virtue-based ethical frameworks? (12.5 marks)

topics.topic_name: Attitude — Content, Structure, Function
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 11 — Attitude; NCERT Class 11 Psychology Ch 6 — Attitude and Social Cognition
key_concepts: Attitude defined — evaluative tendency towards persons, objects, ideas
Three-component model (ABC): Affective, Behavioural, Cognitive
Affective component — feelings and emotions associated with object
Behavioural component — predisposition to act in certain way
Cognitive component — beliefs and knowledge about object
Attitude vs opinion — opinion is specific, attitude is general evaluative stance
Attitude vs value — values are deeper, more stable than attitudes
Functions of attitude: knowledge, ego-defensive, utilitarian, value-expressive
Attitude strength — importance, certainty, accessibility, direct experience
Attitude-behaviour gap — low income but high environmental values
Theory of Planned Behaviour — attitude + subjective norm + perceived control
Measurement: Likert scale, semantic differential, implicit association test
Implicit vs explicit attitudes — unconscious biases
Formation through classical conditioning, operant conditioning, observational learning
Attitude as predictor of public service behaviour
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — asked in every exam since 2014

topics.topic_name: Attitude Formation and Influence on Thought and Behaviour
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 11 Section B; Subba Rao Ch 8 — Attitude Development
key_concepts: Direct personal experience as strongest attitude shaper
Mere exposure effect — familiarity breeds positive evaluation
Social influence — conformity, compliance, obedience in attitude formation
Milgram's obedience experiment — implications for bureaucratic ethics
Media influence — agenda-setting, priming, framing effects on attitudes
Peer group influence — adolescence and attitude crystallisation
Authoritarian personality — attitude syndrome (Adorno)
Stereotyping — overgeneralised attitudes about groups
Prejudice — negative attitude without sufficient justification
Discrimination — behavioural expression of prejudice
Contact hypothesis — Allport's theory for reducing prejudice
Cognitive dissonance — Festinger's theory of attitude-behaviour inconsistency
Self-perception theory — inferring attitudes from own behaviour
Attitude influence on perception — confirmation bias
Civil servant attitude towards marginalised groups — case study relevance

topics.topic_name: Moral and Political Attitudes — Social Influence and Persuasion
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 12 — Persuasion and Attitude Change; Subba Rao Ch 9
key_concepts: Persuasion defined — intentional change of attitude through communication
Elaboration Likelihood Model — central route (logic) vs peripheral route (heuristics)
Source credibility — expertise and trustworthiness in message acceptance
Message characteristics — one-sided vs two-sided, emotional vs rational appeals
Fear appeals — effectiveness and ethics of using fear for behaviour change
Audience factors — need for cognition, prior knowledge, personal relevance
Resistance to persuasion — reactance theory, attitude inoculation
Propaganda vs persuasion — distinction in democratic societies
Political attitudes — spectrum from conservative to liberal, not just left-right
Attitude change in public policy — nudge theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
Social norms marketing — changing behaviour through perceived social norms
Ethics of persuasion — when does persuasion become manipulation?
Attitude change in civil servants — training, exposure, leadership example
Role of political leadership in shaping citizens' political attitudes
Media literacy as defence against manipulative persuasion

sample_questions: What is attitude? Explain the three-component model of attitude with examples. (10 marks)
How does social influence shape attitude formation? Discuss the role of media in this process. (12.5 marks)
Case Study: A senior IAS officer holds stereotypical attitudes towards tribals while implementing a forest rights scheme. How should this be addressed? (20 marks)

modules.module_name: Aptitude and Foundational Values for Civil Services

topics.topic_name: Civil Services — Integrity, Impartiality, Non-Partisanship
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 15 — Civil Services Values; 2nd ARC Report 4 — Chapter 5 on Public Service Values
key_concepts: Integrity defined — wholeness of character, alignment of thought-word-action
Integrity vs honesty — integrity is broader (consistency), honesty is truthfulness
Intellectual integrity — following evidence even when uncomfortable
Financial integrity — no personal gain from public position
Impartiality — equal treatment regardless of personal connection
Non-partisanship — serving government of the day without political bias
Political neutrality — distinct from non-partisanship (no party affiliation)
Objectivity — evidence-based decision-making free from prejudice
Dedication to public service — service orientation over career advancement
Empathy — understanding citizen perspective especially for vulnerable groups
Tolerance and compassion — dealing with diversity in multicultural democracy
Commitment to public interest — Nolan Principles of public life
Selflessness — not acting for personal gain
Openness — willingness to change based on evidence
Leadership in civil services — values expected of IAS, IPS, IFS officers
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — core topic, asked every year

topics.topic_name: Dedication to Public Service and Tolerance
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 16 — Public Service Ethic; Subba Rao Ch 12
key_concepts: Public service motivation (PSM) — Perry's four dimensions
Attraction to policy-making vs civic duty vs compassion vs self-sacrifice
Servant leadership — Robert Greenleaf, leader as servant of followers
Civil servant as trustee — not owner of power but trustee for citizens
Accountability mechanisms — parliamentary, judicial, executive, social
Welfare state rationale — duty of care to citizens who cannot help themselves
Last mile delivery — ethical imperative to reach most marginalised
Tolerance in plural democracy — managing diversity ethically
Multiculturalism vs assimilation — policy ethics in pluralist state
Religious neutrality of civil servants — secularism as professional value
Compassion without paternalism — respecting agency of beneficiaries
Capacity building as service — empowering rather than creating dependency
Work ethic in civil services — punctuality, diligence, commitment
Public trust as social capital — bureaucratic corruption destroys trust
Ethics of discretion — when to use wide vs narrow discretion

topics.topic_name: Commitment to Constitutional Values — Social Justice, Equality, Rule of Law
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 17 — Constitutional Values; 2nd ARC Report 4
key_concepts: Constitutional morality — Ambedkar's concept, letter vs spirit of Constitution
Rule of law — Dicey's three elements: supremacy of law, equality before law, conventions
Social justice as constitutional mandate — Article 46, DPSP framework
Equity vs equality — same treatment vs proportionate treatment
Restorative justice — repairing harm vs punitive approaches
Distributive justice — fair allocation of resources and opportunities
Procedural justice — fair process even if outcome not always ideal
Fundamental rights as ethical floor — non-derogable minimum
Directive Principles — policy ethics framework for welfare state
Article 21 expansive reading — dignity, livelihood, health as rights
Anti-corruption as constitutional value — Article 311 (protection with accountability)
Representation of marginalised — ethical imperative in democratic governance
Environmental ethics in Constitution — Article 51A(g), Article 48A
Federal ethics — centre-state relations and cooperative federalism values
Civil servant oath — constitutional commitment and its ethical implications

sample_questions: What are the foundational values expected of a civil servant? Elaborate on integrity and impartiality with examples. (12.5 marks)
Explain constitutional morality as a civil service value. How does it differ from popular morality? (10 marks)
Case Study: An IAS officer receives political pressure to clear an environmental clearance for a mining project in a tribal area. What should she do? (25 marks)

topics.topic_name: Emotional Intelligence — Concepts and Components
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 13 — Emotional Intelligence; NCERT Class 11 Psychology Ch 2 — Self and Personality
key_concepts: EI defined — ability to recognise, understand, manage emotions in self and others
IQ vs EQ debate — Goleman's claim that EQ predicts 80% of success
Four-branch model — Mayer, Salovey, Caruso: perceiving, using, understanding, managing
Goleman's five components: self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, social skills
Self-awareness — knowing one's own emotions, strengths, limitations
Self-regulation — managing disruptive emotions and impulses
Intrinsic motivation — drive to achieve for its own sake, not external rewards
Empathy — reading others' emotional states accurately
Social skills — managing relationships, building rapport, influence
EI and leadership effectiveness — transformational leadership requires high EQ
EI in conflict resolution — calming disputes, finding common ground
EI in stress management — coping with high-pressure bureaucratic environments
EI and decision-making — avoiding emotion-driven bad decisions while using emotional info
EI training — can it be developed? Evidence for structured training
EI in UPSC context — dealing with distressed citizens, team management
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — asked almost every year since 2016

topics.topic_name: EI Utilisation in Administration and Governance
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 13 Section B; Subba Rao Ch 10 — EI in Civil Services
key_concepts: Crisis management — EI for maintaining composure under pressure
Grievance redressal — empathetic listening transforms adversarial interactions
Community engagement — building trust with rural, tribal, marginalised communities
Inter-departmental coordination — social skills to manage turf battles
Corruption resistance — self-regulation resisting temptation
Whistleblowing decision — emotional courage vs fear of consequences
EI in negotiation — reading counterpart's emotional state
Performance management — empathetic feedback to subordinates
EI and burnout prevention — self-awareness of emotional depletion
Diversity management — empathy across cultural, linguistic, gender lines
EI and media relations — maintaining dignity under hostile questioning
Disaster response — EI for dealing with traumatised affected populations
EI and police-public relations — de-escalation vs force escalation
EI in judicial administration — empathy without compromising impartiality
Building institutional EI — organisational culture that values emotional competence

sample_questions: What is Emotional Intelligence? Explain Goleman's model and its relevance for civil servants. (12.5 marks)
Case Study: A District Collector is overseeing relief camps after a flood. Survivors are angry and shouting at officials. How should the EI of the Collector help manage this situation? (25 marks)

modules.module_name: Contributions of Moral Thinkers — India and World

topics.topic_name: Indian Moral Thinkers — Ancient and Medieval
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 14 — Indian Ethical Traditions; Subba Rao Ch 6
key_concepts: Kautilya (Arthashastra) — statecraft ethics, welfare state, anti-corruption measures
Kautilya's punishment theory — graduated deterrence, proportionality
Kautilya's officer conduct — inspection, accountability, salary to prevent corruption
Buddha — Four Noble Truths, Eightfold Path, Middle Way, compassion (karuna)
Ahimsa in Buddhist ethics — harm minimisation in governance
Mahavira — anekantavada (many-sidedness), syadvada, ahimsa as universal principle
Jain ethics — non-possessiveness (aparigraha) as governance value
Adi Shankaracharya — advaita, universal consciousness, service as worship
Ramanuja — visishtadvaita, devotion with social action
Basavanna — vachana movement, anti-caste, dignity of labour
Kabir — syncretic ethics, critique of religious hypocrisy, universal brotherhood
Tukaram and Mirabai — bhakti ethics, equality before god
Chanakya vs Buddha — realist statecraft vs compassionate governance tension
Dharmashastra tradition — dharma as cosmic and social order
Upanishadic ethics — aham brahmasmi, treating others as self
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — at least one Indian thinker question per exam

topics.topic_name: Indian Moral Thinkers — Modern
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 14 Section C; Subba Rao Ch 7
key_concepts: Mahatma Gandhi — satyagraha, ahimsa, truth, trusteeship theory of property
Gandhi's seven social sins — politics without principles, wealth without work, etc.
Talisman test — visualising the poorest person before every decision
Swami Vivekananda — seva as worship, practical vedanta, dignity of the poor
Daridra Narayana — serving the poor as serving god
B.R. Ambedkar — constitutional morality, annihilation of caste, social democracy
Liberty-equality-fraternity as inseparable trinity (Ambedkar)
Rabindranath Tagore — humanist ethics, education for whole person, Gitanjali values
Tagore's critique of narrow nationalism — universal human solidarity
Sri Aurobindo — integral yoga, divine humanity, evolutionary ethics
Vinoba Bhave — Bhoodan movement, voluntary ethics over coercive redistribution
Jayaprakash Narayan — total revolution, ethics of political participation
Rammohun Roy — rationalist ethics, social reform, widow remarriage
Gopal Krishna Gokhale — moderate reform, constitutional ethics
Lal Bahadur Shastri — simplicity, accountability, Jai Jawan Jai Kisan as values
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — Gandhi and Ambedkar asked in nearly every exam

topics.topic_name: Western Moral Thinkers
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 6-10 (thinker-specific sections); Subba Rao Ch 5
key_concepts: Socrates — examined life, moral knowledge, intellectual humility
Plato — philosopher king, justice as harmony, allegory of the cave
Aristotle — eudaimonia, virtue as habit, practical wisdom (phronesis)
Epicurus — pleasure as absence of pain, simple life, friendship
Stoics (Marcus Aurelius, Epictetus) — virtue, reason, indifference to externals
Thomas Aquinas — natural law, divine law, reason and faith synthesis
John Locke — natural rights, government by consent, property theory
David Hume — moral sentimentalism, sympathy as basis of ethics
Immanuel Kant — duty, categorical imperative, human dignity
Jeremy Bentham — utility, hedonistic calculus, felicific calculus
John Stuart Mill — higher and lower pleasures, harm principle, women's rights
Karl Marx — historical materialism, alienation, class-based ethics
John Rawls — veil of ignorance, difference principle, justice as fairness
Peter Singer — expanding circle, global poverty as moral issue
Jürgen Habermas — discourse ethics, communicative rationality, public sphere
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — Kant, Bentham, Mill, Rawls, Aristotle all tested

sample_questions: Compare Kautilya's approach to governance ethics with Gandhi's trusteeship theory. Which is more relevant for modern India? (12.5 marks)
What lessons can modern civil servants draw from the ethical teachings of Buddha and Mahavira? (10 marks)
Explain Rawls' theory of justice and its application to India's social justice policies. (12.5 marks)

modules.module_name: Public/Civil Service Values and Ethics in Public Administration; Probity in Governance

topics.topic_name: Status and Problems of Ethics in Public Administration
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 15-16; 2nd ARC Report 4 — Ethics in Governance (full report)
key_concepts: Public administration ethics distinct from private sector ethics
Democratic ethos — citizens as principals, officials as agents
Ethical failures in administration — bribery, nepotism, misuse of office
Causes of corruption — low salaries, discretionary power, lack of accountability
Systemic vs individual corruption — structural vs personal failure
Ethics infrastructure — codes of conduct, training, oversight, whistleblower protection
Locke's spoils system vs merit-based civil services
Bureaucratic pathologies — red tape, buck-passing, decision avoidance
Ethical climate in organisations — top management tone setting
AIS conduct rules and their ethical rationale
Conflict of interest in public service — types and management
Gifts and hospitality — rules and ethical reasoning
Public-private partnership ethics — revolving door problem
Ethics audit — systematic review of ethical conduct in departments
CVC, Lokpal, Lokayukta as ethics enforcement institutions

topics.topic_name: Ethical Concerns and Dilemmas in Government and Private Institutions
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 18 — Ethical Dilemmas; Subba Rao Ch 13
key_concepts: Ethical dilemma defined — choice between two or more competing ethical claims
Types: right-vs-right (two goods), right-vs-wrong (obvious), wrong-vs-wrong (tragic)
Truth vs loyalty dilemma — whistleblowing against colleague
Individual vs community dilemma — resettlement of tribals for dams
Short-term vs long-term dilemma — immediate relief vs sustainable development
Justice vs mercy dilemma — rule application vs humanitarian exception
Dilemma resolution frameworks: ends-based, rules-based, care-based
Reflective equilibrium — moving between principles and intuitions
Six-step dilemma resolution: recognise, identify stakeholders, gather facts, identify options, evaluate, decide
Corporate ethics dilemmas — whistleblowing, product safety, environmental compliance
Media ethics dilemmas — public interest vs privacy vs national security
Research ethics — human subjects, consent, data privacy
Medical ethics — four principles: autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice
Police ethics — use of force, interrogation methods, encounter killings
Judicial ethics — impartiality, recusal, independence
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — dilemma questions in Section A and B

topics.topic_name: Probity in Governance — Concept, Components, Measures
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 19 — Probity; 2nd ARC Report 4; Subba Rao Ch 14
key_concepts: Probity defined — adherence to highest standards of integrity and ethics
Probity vs integrity — probity specifically in financial matters, integrity broader
Components: financial propriety, impartiality, accountability, transparency
Financial probity — no misuse of public funds, proper accounts, audit compliance
Code of Ethics vs Code of Conduct — principles vs specific rules
Citizens' Charter — accountability mechanism, service standards
Right to Information Act 2005 — transparency as probity instrument
Whistleblower Protection Act 2014 — probity incentive mechanism
Asset declaration by public servants — accountability of wealth
Vigilance Commission — CVC's role in maintaining probity standards
Audit and accountability — CAG function as probity check
e-Governance as probity tool — reducing human discretion
Social audit — community accountability for public works (MGNREGA)
Probity in procurement — GeM portal, tender integrity protocols
International conventions — UNCAC (UN Convention Against Corruption)
topics.pyq_frequency: High — probity appears in 60%+ of exam papers

topics.topic_name: Work Culture, Quality of Service Delivery, Utilisation of Public Funds
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 20; 2nd ARC Report 12 — Citizen Centric Administration; Subba Rao Ch 15
key_concepts: Work culture in public sector — how organisational culture shapes ethics
New Public Management — performance measurement, results orientation
Good governance as work culture standard
Service delivery ethics — timeliness, courtesy, accuracy, accessibility
Total Quality Management in government — quality circles, process improvement
ISO certification for government departments — quality assurance
Funds utilisation ethics — leakages, diversion, padded estimates
PFMS (Public Financial Management System) — tracking fund flows
Outcome budgeting — linking expenditure to results
Zero-based budgeting — scrutiny of all expenditures ethically
Audit reports as accountability tools — PAC scrutiny
Anti-corruption measures — decoy operations, surprise inspections
Incentive structures — performance pay, fast-track promotions for integrity
Negative incentives — public naming, prosecution for fund misuse
Citizen feedback mechanisms — satisfaction surveys, social media redressal

topics.topic_name: Corporate Governance — Accountability and Ethical Issues
topics.primary_source: Lexicon: Chapter 21 — Corporate Governance; Subba Rao Ch 16
key_concepts: Corporate governance defined — system of rules, practices, processes for directing companies
Stakeholder theory — Friedman vs Freeman on corporate purpose
CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility) — ethical obligation vs legal compliance
Board independence — preventing management capture of oversight
Audit committee — integrity of financial reporting
Insider trading — using privileged information unethically
Executive compensation ethics — CEO pay vs worker wages
Environmental governance — greenwashing vs genuine sustainability
Corporate fraud — Enron, Satyam scandal lessons
Whistleblowing in corporations — Sarbanes-Oxley protections
Supply chain ethics — labour standards, child labour, worker rights
ESG (Environmental Social Governance) — new corporate accountability framework
SEBI and corporate ethics — regulatory framework
Companies Act 2013 — CSR mandate, independent directors provisions
Public sector enterprise ethics — CIL, ONGC accountability to citizens

sample_questions: What is probity in governance? Discuss the measures needed to ensure probity in the public sector. (12.5 marks)
Examine the ethical dilemmas faced by civil servants in India. How should they resolve conflicts between personal ethics and official duty? (12.5 marks)

Case Study: An IAS officer discovers that funds meant for a tribal welfare scheme have been diverted by a junior official who is also the officer's close friend. What ethical issues arise and what course of action should be taken? (25 marks)

modules.module_name: Case Studies Framework — Methodology and Practice
modules.note: This module is crucial — 60% of GS4 marks come from case studies. Practice daily with diverse scenarios.

topics.topic_name: Case Study Methodology — How to Approach and Structure Answers
topics.primary_source: Subba Rao Ch 17-20 — Case Studies; UPSC Previous Year Case Studies 2013-2024
key_concepts: Case study structure: Introduction → Stakeholder mapping → Ethical issues → Options → Decision + Justification
Step 1: Identify the central ethical conflict clearly in 2-3 sentences
Step 2: Map ALL stakeholders and their interests/rights
Step 3: Identify which ethical principles are in tension
Step 4: Generate at least 3 courses of action
Step 5: Evaluate each option against legal, ethical, practical criteria
Step 6: Choose and justify with ethical theory + practical constraints
UPSC expectations: decisive answer, multiple perspectives, awareness of consequences
Avoid 'both sides' fence-sitting — UPSC wants civil servant decisiveness
Length: typically 400-600 words per case study (25 marks)
Use of examples and precedents to support decisions
Institutional mechanisms to invoke — disciplinary action, RTI, CAT, CVC
Balance — don't be so idealistic as to be impractical
Emotional intelligence in answer — show empathy in stakeholder analysis
Format: use paragraphs, not bullet points for case study answers
topics.pyq_frequency: Very High — half the paper is case studies

topics.topic_name: Category 1 — Corruption and Conflict of Interest Cases
topics.primary_source: Subba Rao Ch 18; UPSC 2014, 2016, 2018 Case Studies
key_concepts: Bribery scenarios — direct demand vs implied expectation
Gift vs bribe — when a gift becomes a bribe
Conflict of interest — family connections in contracts
Asset misuse — using government vehicle, staff for personal work
Nepotism in postings and transfers
Speed money — small facilitating payments in permits
Kickback in procurement — commission demanded by official
Prevention vs reporting dilemma — stop ongoing corruption vs collect evidence
Reporting superior's corruption — risk vs duty
Anonymous tip about colleague — how to verify and act
Corruption with development outcomes — corrupt official delivers results
Systemic corruption — single official cannot stop it
Political protection for corrupt subordinate
Use of RTI/audit findings to counter corruption
Personal dilemma: family in financial crisis, temptation to accept bribe

topics.topic_name: Category 2 — Welfare and Rights-Based Scenarios
topics.primary_source: Subba Rao Ch 19; UPSC 2015, 2017, 2019 Case Studies
key_concepts: Displacement and rehabilitation — tribal, farmer land acquisition dilemmas
Scheme benefits vs eligibility — helping deserving but technically ineligible person
Last mile delivery failure — remote villagers not receiving entitlements
Gender discrimination in beneficiary selection — local official prejudice
Disability inclusion in scheme implementation
Child labour in project — contractor employing minors
Bonded labour rescue — safety of official vs duty to victim
LGBTQ+ rights in official capacity — personal vs constitutional values
Caste-based denial of services — confronting entrenched discrimination
Right to food vs budgetary constraints
Healthcare access dilemma — rule vs life-saving emergency
School mid-day meal quality versus cost cutting by contractor
Flood relief discrimination by local leaders
Old age pension denial due to documentation gaps
Medical ethics in rural postings — resource allocation triage

topics.topic_name: Category 3 — Whistleblowing, Loyalty, and Institutional Integrity
topics.primary_source: Subba Rao Ch 20; UPSC 2020-2024 Case Studies
key_concepts: Whistleblowing process — gather evidence, use internal channels first
Whistleblower Protection Act 2014 — procedure and protections
Loyalty to institution vs loyalty to truth
Solidarity with colleague vs institutional integrity
Leaking to media — when is it justified?
Anonymous complaint vs named complaint — trade-offs
Retaliation against whistleblower — how to respond
Politicised investigation — pressure to drop case
Maintaining confidentiality while seeking redress
Digital evidence — using mobile recordings ethically and legally
Hierarchy pressure — superior ordering suppression of report
Transfer as punishment for speaking up
Courts as last resort — filing writ petition vs exhausting administrative remedies
Internal audit findings — duty to report vs cover-up pressure
Post-retirement whistleblowing — no protection but perhaps greater duty

topics.topic_name: Category 4 — Law, Discretion, and Justice Scenarios
topics.primary_source: Subba Rao Ch 21; UPSC 2013, 2021, 2022 Case Studies
key_concepts: Strict law vs equity — when letter of law gives unjust result
Discretion to waive penalties in genuine hardship
Emergency clause use — extraordinary situations requiring extraordinary action
Arrest vs warning for minor offence by vulnerable person
Environmental law enforcement vs livelihood of fishermen/miners
Eviction versus livelihood — encroachment by poor vs court order
Police use of force ethics — minimum necessary force principle
Encounter killing ethics — national security vs judicial process
Custody deaths — investigation against own colleagues
Bail application in sensitive case — pressure from both sides
Disaster relief prioritisation — who gets resources first
Triage ethics — mass casualty incident, limited medical resources
Anticipatory bail and its ethics in organised crime cases
Confidential informant ethics — protecting source vs disclosure
IPC vs customary law — tribal justice systems and formal law tension

sample_questions: You are the DM. During a flood, you have resources to save either a group of 40 elderly persons OR a group of 10 children. You cannot save both. What do you do and why? (25 marks)
An officer discovers that her departmental superior is involved in a scam worth Rs 5 crore, and the superior has also helped her career in the past. What should she do? (25 marks)
A forest officer is ordered to approve a road through a tiger reserve for political reasons. The road would fragment the corridor. What course of action should he take? (25 marks)

daily_routine.theory_reading: 1.5 hours — Lexicon primary + Subba Rao supplementary
daily_routine.answer_writing: 1 hour — one 10-mark answer or half of one 25-mark case study
daily_routine.revision: 30 minutes — flashcards of thinkers and key definitions
daily_routine.case_study_practice: 1 hour — one full case study analysis per day from Module 8

phase_5.focus: Case study practice — parallel with all other phases from week 3
phase_5.note: Case study practice should BEGIN in week 3, not only after completing theory

high_value_topics_for_quick_revision: Gandhi's seven social sins + talisman test
Kant's categorical imperative — all three formulations
Nolan's seven principles of public life
Rawls' veil of ignorance + difference principle
Steps to resolve ethical dilemma (6-step model)
Probity components — financial, impartiality, transparency, accountability

output_format.daily_target: topic_id + specific Lexicon chapters + key definitions to memorise
output_format.answer_writing_prompt: one question from topic to write each day

ai_module_generator_schema.module_test_trigger: after completing all topics in module, auto-generate from sample_questions bank
ai_module_generator_schema.fault_analysis: compare student answer to model answer → flag missing ethical theory citations, missing stakeholder identification, vague conclusions
